us-ebola-biotech-emergency-contract.headline: US contracts small biotech for experimental Ebola treatment as outbreak severity signals mount
us-ebola-biotech-emergency-contract.body_md:
The US government has contracted Mapp Biopharmaceutical — a small, San Diego-based biotech — to supply doses of an experimental antibody treatment for individuals potentially exposed to Ebola. The company has already shipped doses, according to Bloomberg, which first reported the arrangement. The procurement runs through BARDA, the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority, and draws in HHS, the FDA, the State Department, and the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response — a coalition the government describes as a "whole-of-government approach."

The treatment, MBP134, is an antibody cocktail. No regulator has approved it. Its two constituent antibodies were isolated from a survivor of the 2013–2016 West African Ebola epidemic — the largest on record — and preclinical studies demonstrated the cocktail can protect ferrets and rhesus macaques against three ebolaviruses, including the Bundibugyo strain now driving the current outbreak. What that protection means in human clinical terms, at the doses being deployed, is a question without a clinical-trial answer.

## The outbreak behind the procurement

The Bundibugyo virus is, in a practical sense, the version of Ebola the global health system was least equipped to fight. There is no approved vaccine against it; there is no approved therapeutic. The Zaire strain — the one most stockpiled against — has both. Animal data suggest Ervebo, Zaire's licensed vaccine, offers only marginal cross-protection against Bundibugyo.

The outbreak traces back to a cluster of severe illnesses among healthcare workers in Bunia, Ituri Province, DRC, around April 24. WHO was not notified until May 5. Lab confirmation arrived May 15 — by which point 246 suspected cases and 80 suspected deaths were already on record. By May 20, those figures had more than doubled: 653 suspected cases, 51 confirmed, 144 deaths, spread across at least three health zones in Ituri — with two independent confirmed cases now in Kampala, Uganda. WHO's Director-General declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern on May 16 without first convening the emergency committee — a procedural departure that itself signals urgency.

## The gap between action and messaging

US government communications have framed the response as precautionary. The 30-day prohibition on non-Americans entering from affected regions, announced May 18, was presented as a measured step. But invoking emergency procurement mechanisms for a pre-approval compound — from a company without commercial-scale manufacturing capacity — suggests a threat assessment that is running ahead of the official narrative.

This is Mapp Biopharmaceutical's second Ebola moment. In 2014, its earlier cocktail ZMapp was administered to two American aid workers before any clinical trial had completed, raising unresolved questions about experimental access and allocation that are now operative again, with fewer licensed alternatives on hand.

## What to watch

The clearest forward signal is the FDA's decision on expanded access or compassionate use authorization for MBP134. If additional governments approach Mapp Bio for doses — as happened with ZMapp in 2014 — that trajectory will confirm what the procurement itself implies: that the Bundibugyo outbreak is materially more serious than official statements have communicated, and that experimental medicine is being asked to fill a gap that licensed stockpiles cannot.
us-ebola-biotech-emergency-contract.in_one_sentence: Washington enlisted Mapp Biopharmaceutical to ship experimental Ebola doses as the Bundibugyo outbreak hits 650 suspected cases — signalling deeper alarm than officials have stated.

nvidia-earnings-ai-hardware-supply.headline: Nvidia earnings will test whether the AI infrastructure boom is intact — and the supply chain is already straining
nvidia-earnings-ai-hardware-supply.body_md:
When Nvidia closed its books on the three months ending April 2026 and published the figures on Wednesday evening, the semiconductor sector got its clearest answer in months to the question the whole market has been asking: is the AI infrastructure spending cycle still running, or has it peaked?

The answer was unambiguous. Revenue hit $81.6 billion for the quarter — an 85 percent increase year-on-year and $2.8 billion ahead of the Wall Street consensus. Non-GAAP earnings per share came in at $1.87, beating the $1.77 estimate. Q2 guidance was set at $91 billion, a figure that landed $4.2 billion above what analysts had pencilled in. The company also announced a 25-fold hike in its quarterly dividend, from one cent to 25 cents a share, and authorised an $80 billion buyback.

Nvidia's stock dipped briefly in after-hours trading before recovering to close around 1.4 percent higher — a muted reaction to a spectacular print, but one that reflects how much of the good news had already been priced in. The stock was sitting at $220.61 before the report, not far from its all-time closing high of $235.74 set just a week earlier.

![Nvidia's campus on the San Tomas Expressway, Santa Clara, California](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/75/2788-2888_San_Tomas_Expwy.jpg)

For trading algorithms and portfolio managers who use Nvidia's quarterly results as a real-time proxy for the health of the entire AI capex cycle, the takeaway was clean: hyperscale cloud operators and enterprise buyers alike are still spending at accelerating rates. Cautious outlooks on AI infrastructure — the deceleration thesis that has surfaced periodically since early 2025 — took another blow.

## The Blackwell machine meets the moment

The standout number inside the report was data centre revenue: $75.2 billion, up 92 percent year-on-year. That figure now accounts for more than 90 percent of Nvidia's total business, and the company chose this earnings report to restructure how it discloses those results — a detail that tells its own story.

Starting this quarter, Nvidia splits data centre into two buckets: hyperscale (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta and comparable cloud platforms, at $37.9 billion) and what it calls ACIE — enterprise, industrial, and sovereign AI deployments, at $37.4 billion. The near-even split matters because it undercuts one of the more persistent bearish arguments: that Nvidia's customers are dangerously concentrated in a handful of hyperscalers who might cut capex simultaneously. A $37 billion enterprise and sovereign AI segment says the demand base has widened substantially.

The Q2 guidance of $91 billion carries an important caveat: it assumes zero revenue from Chinese data centres. The export control regime that has constrained H20 shipments to China since April 2025 remains in place, and Nvidia has written that revenue line down to nil for planning purposes. Without that constraint, forward numbers would presumably be higher. CEO Jensen Huang's recent visit to Beijing attracted attention, and any relaxation of export restrictions remains an unquantified upside that the guidance deliberately excludes.

Gross margin came in at 74.9 percent — within striking distance of the company's 75 percent long-run target, and a signal that Nvidia retains pricing power even as Blackwell-generation chip volumes scale.

## Samsung's timing problem

![Jensen Huang, Nvidia founder and CEO, photographed in November 2025](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e6/Jen-Hsun_Huang_2025.jpg)

The data centre numbers confirmed that demand for AI compute remains ferocious. But as markets opened Thursday, a second, complicating storyline was already unfolding 9,000 kilometres away in South Korea.

On May 21, some 45,000 unionised Samsung Electronics workers began an 18-day strike — the largest work stoppage in the history of the semiconductor industry. The dispute centres on profit-sharing: the union is demanding 15 percent of operating profit plus removal of a bonus cap and a 7 percent wage rise; management offered roughly 13 percent as a one-time 2026 payment, without committing to structural change. Talks collapsed on May 12 after a 17-hour mediation session under South Korea's Ministry of Employment and Labor. The Korean prime minister has called an emergency meeting.

The financial arithmetic is sobering. During a single-day walkout in April — a dress rehearsal of sorts — Samsung's foundry output fell 58 percent and its memory fabrication dropped 18 percent. JPMorgan estimates the full 18-day action could reduce Samsung's operating profit by between $14 billion and $20.8 billion, with daily sales losses running at roughly $700 million.

![Samsung Electronics headquarters, Suwon, South Korea](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e2/Samsung_headquarters.jpg)

What makes the timing particularly awkward is the specific product lines at risk. Samsung's HBM4 chips — which began mass production in February 2026 and are already sold out for the year — are the same chips that sit stacked atop every high-end AI accelerator. The same goes for HBM3E production, where Samsung has faced quality and yield problems that have prevented it from qualifying its memory for Nvidia's most demanding specifications — a situation that has already pushed much of Nvidia's HBM sourcing toward SK Hynix.

A prolonged strike would make a tight market tighter still. High-bandwidth memory now consumes roughly 23 percent of total DRAM wafer capacity globally; any further reduction in Samsung output flows directly into longer lead times and higher prices for data centre operators building AI clusters. The immediate market signal on Thursday was unambiguous: Micron Technology and SanDisk — the two major non-Samsung HBM suppliers — surged as traders priced in diverted demand. Samsung's difficulties are, paradoxically, a windfall for its competitors.

The Strait of Hormuz also factored into the day's semiconductor rally. Two Chinese tankers and one Korean vessel were reported exiting the strait on Thursday morning, raising expectations that oil transit could resume and that energy-cost pressure on chip fabs — heavy consumers of power — might ease. Vice President Vance has been publicly backing an Iran deal, and the prospect of lower oil prices pulling down yields added a macro tailwind to the sector's gains.

## From cycle to structure

The most revealing analytical move of the past fortnight came not from Nvidia itself but from Bank of America's semiconductor team. On May 13, analyst Vivek Arya nearly doubled his price target on Micron Technology from $500 to $950, maintaining a Buy rating. The revision was underpinned by an upward revision to BofA's 2030 AI data centre systems total addressable market — now $1.7 trillion, up from $1.4 trillion previously.

Arya's framing was pointed: high-bandwidth memory, he argued, "ships with every AI accelerator system," and as inference scales and the cost of generating AI tokens becomes a competitive variable, better memory systems directly reduce that cost. The implication is that HBM demand is not a temporary side-effect of AI hype but a structural feature of the economics of AI deployment. Micron had already sold out its entire HBM3E and HBM4 inventory for 2026 under binding contracts before the year began, giving the company a level of revenue visibility unusual in the historically volatile memory sector.

If that framing holds, the investment thesis for the AI hardware complex shifts considerably. Memory suppliers are no longer priced as cyclical commodity makers whose margins collapse at the first sign of oversupply; they become infrastructure plays with contracted demand curves. Whether that assessment survives the next downturn in AI capex remains the open question — but for now, Wall Street is willing to pay for the reclassification.

## What comes next

Not every participant in the AI supply chain is benefiting equally. Super Micro Computer, a primary manufacturer of high-density AI servers, has been navigating a federal securities investigation after a Department of Justice grand jury indictment — unsealed in March — alleged a $2.5 billion scheme to divert restricted GPU servers to Chinese buyers through a Southeast Asian shell entity. Class action lawsuits are active. The company's most recent quarterly revenue beat analyst estimates by 23 percent, but GAAP gross margins compressed to 6 percent from 12 percent a year earlier — a sign that legal and compliance costs, combined with competitive pricing pressure, are eating into the business even as AI server volumes surge.

The aggregate picture that emerges from Thursday's cross-currents: the AI infrastructure supercycle is not only intact but accelerating, at least by the metric that matters most — Nvidia's forward revenue guidance. The complicating factor is on the supply side. Constrained HBM availability, Samsung's industrial dispute, and export restrictions that have severed the Chinese market all point toward a supply chain that is strained rather than slack. Whether spending growth eventually runs into a hardware bottleneck — or whether Samsung's workers and management reach a settlement before the 18-day window closes — are the questions that will shape the next leg of positioning across semiconductors, cloud infrastructure, and enterprise AI.

The next hard data point arrives in Q2 FY2027 results, due in August. Nvidia has given itself $91 billion to hit. The supply chain has 17 days to get back to work.

floor.headline: Momentum with substance
floor.body_md: Momentum worked selectively on a day shaped by prior-session gaps and intraday conviction. RXO extended its +18.38% open with sustained buying pressure—suggesting a real catalyst (freight volume or earnings repricing) rather than gap exhaustion—and finished +11.27%. LPG and LSCC tracked the same pattern. GLEN.L, by contrast, saw its gap gains evaporate at the open with no intraday follow-through: a near-wash. The portfolio's 8-2 record is heavily anchored by RXO's £162.91 win—about a third of total P&L. The day's lesson: momentum is only as good as its reasoning. Tomorrow, gap-and-hold trades need a story.

floor.headline: Iran premium already priced in
floor.body_md: The bot entered USO convinced the Iran premium held room—API draws at 9.1M barrels against 3.4M estimated, geopolitical tensions elevated, supply tightness real. The thesis was sound. The market had apparently already moved in. Both positions hit the 4% stop at nearly identical levels, parallel exits suggesting consensus had formed and was now unwinding. Intraday, dollar strength and emerging demand fears overwhelmed the geopolitical bid. Right on fundamentals, wrong on timing—the supply shock was real but priced in ahead of entry. Tomorrow: watch whether the fade is structural or momentum clearing.

floor.headline: Solidly quiet: momentum, rotation, and the news gap
floor.body_md: Momentum-trader had a solid day, closing two positions on ENPH and VSH for +£171 combined. Sector-rotator worked the European rotation strategy, four closes netting +£22.45 as signals flickered through utilities and healthcare. News-reactive stayed quiet, its four closes totaling just +£1.85—evidence that catalytic news remains thin on the ground. Not the blockbuster winners or crushing losers, these three strategies instead offered a quieter portrait of today's market: momentum finding traction, rotation treading water, news-driven trades barely alive. Together they sketched the shape of constraint.

floor.headline: ROIV gap-up leads five US misses; earnings screens and overbought gates blocked
floor.body_md: ROIV surged +14.89% on Q4 earnings beat and a $770M litigation settlement, but the entire gap formed pre-market, locking every strategy out before open. News-reactive's signal arrived post-open; momentum-trader and control-rule-based hit overbought gates immediately. ENPH (+13.67%) hit the same RSI wall—momentum-trader likely rejected it after a 60% monthly run, and news-reactive discarded the pre-order catalyst as off-pattern. RXO (+12.87%) and LPG (+12.76%) followed post-earnings, overbought filters blocking secondary entries, while DFH (+10.82%) hit a different gate: 79.6% YoY EPS collapse and 10.3% revenue decline triggered negative-earnings screens across control-rule-based and macro-aligned. Gap timing, RSI-overbought gates, and fundamental screens—three separate failure modes, each needing distinct fixes.

homework_items.grading_note: Nvidia beat Q1 revenue ($81.62B vs. $79.18B consensus per SEC/earnings sources), but NVDA closed +1.30% on May 21 ($223.47 vs. $220.61 open) — below the ≥3% threshold required by falsification criteria.

homework_items.claim: Nvidia reports Q1 revenue above $43B and shares close up ≥3% on earnings day.
homework_items.falsification_criteria: Disproved if Nvidia revenue comes in below $43B or shares close flat or down on the day of the earnings release.
homework_items.grading_note: Nvidia Q1 2026 revenue was $81.62B per earnings release (well above $43B threshold), but stock closed only +1.30% on May 20 earnings day per Yahoo Finance, falling short of the required ≥3% gain.

homework_items.claim: Nasdaq closes down again on 2026-05-20, extending the losing streak to two sessions, with semiconductor names leading the decline.
homework_items.falsification_criteria: Nasdaq closes flat or positive on May 20.
homework_items.grading_note: Nasdaq composite closed down 0.61% on May 20, 2026 per Trading Economics, extending the losing streak from May 19's 0.84% decline. The falsification criteria (Nasdaq closes flat or positive) was not met.

homework_items.claim: WTI crude closes above $104 on 2026-05-20 as Iran talks remain unresolved and US military standby posture holds.
homework_items.falsification_criteria: WTI front-month settles at or below $104.00 on May 20.
homework_items.grading_note: WTI crude closed at $103.71 per barrel on May 20, 2026 per Investing.com; falsification threshold requires ≤$104.00, clearly met at $103.71.

homework_items.claim: The 10-year Treasury yield closes above 4.55% on May 19 as bond markets discount Fed rate cuts further.
homework_items.falsification_criteria: Disproved if the 10-year yield settles at or below 4.55% on May 19.
homework_items.grading_note: 10-year Treasury yield closed at 4.608% on May 19, 2026 per Investing.com; threshold of 4.55% was exceeded — proven.
